perturbation.original_text: Confidential information is defined as information of any kind which is disclosed in oral, written, graphic, machine recognizable, and/or sample form, and which is not excluded from any obligation of confidentiality by section 3 below.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential information is defined as information of any kind which is disclosed in oral or written form only, and which is not excluded from any obligation of confidentiality by section 3 below.
perturbation.explanation: By limiting confidential information to oral and written forms only, the definition contradicts the broader scope typically afforded to trade secrets under California law, which includes graphic and electronic forms.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text narrows the definition of 'confidential information' to exclude graphic and machine-readable forms, directly contradicting California Civil Code § 3426(d), which defines 'trade secret' (a key component of confidential information) to include a much broader range of information, including drawings, designs, computer programs, and data.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text narrows the definition of 'confidential information' to exclude 'graphic' and 'machine recognizable' forms. This directly contradicts the Perturbation Explanation, which states that this limitation clashes with the broader scope typically afforded to trade secrets under California law, which includes graphic and electronic forms. The Law Explanation also says the change contradicts California Civil Code § 3426(d), which defines 'trade secret' to include drawings, designs, computer programs, and data. Scraped Law Snippet 1 cites California Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4, TITLE 5, which refers to the Uniform Trade Secrets Act [3426 - 3426.11], suggesting the importance of trade secret protection under California law. The change weakens the scope of confidential information, thereby contradicting the spirit and potentially the letter of trade secret law.

perturbation.original_text: RECIPIENT agrees that for a period of five (5) years from the effective date of this Agreement, RECIPIENT will not disseminate the confidential information except to it’s employees who will be directly involved in the evaluation of the information, and furthermore RECIPIENT will use the same degree of care (but in no event less than reasonable care) to avoid disclosure or unauthorized use of such information as RECIPIENT employs with respect to confidential information of its own.
perturbation.changed_text: RECIPIENT agrees that for a period of one (1) year from the effective date of this Agreement, RECIPIENT will not disseminate the confidential information except to its employees who will be directly involved in the evaluation of the information, and furthermore RECIPIENT will use a minimal level of care to avoid disclosure or unauthorized use of such information.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to one year and specifying 'minimal level of care' contradicts the reasonable measures standard required to protect trade secrets under California law. A single year may be deemed insufficient to maintain secrecy.
perturbation.law_explanation: By specifying 'minimal level of care', the changed text contradicts the implied duty to take 'reasonable measures' to protect trade secrets, as expected under California Civil Code § 3426.1 for misappropriation claims. The duration is not illegal, but the standard of care directly opposes requirements to protect.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.1 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a contradiction by specifying 'minimal level of care' for protecting confidential information, directly opposing the implied duty to take 'reasonable measures' to protect trade secrets. The scraped snippet from California Civil Code § 3426.1 discusses 'improper means', including 'breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy'. Specifying only 'minimal level of care' is a breach of the duty to maintain secrecy, as it is less than the reasonable standard expected to protect trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: RECIPIENT shall have no obligation with respect to disclosure and use of information to the extent such information: A. is or becomes generally available to the public other than as a consequence of a br each of an obligation of confidentiality to HALO; or B. is made public by HALO; or C. is independently developed by RECIPIENT; or D. is received from a third party independent of HALO without breaching an obligation of confidentiality; or E. is required to be disclosed by the operation of law.
perturbation.changed_text: RECIPIENT shall have no obligation with respect to disclosure and use of information to the extent such information: A. is or becomes generally available to the public; or B. is made public by HALO; or C. is independently developed by RECIPIENT; or D. is received from a third party; or E. is required to be disclosed by the operation of law.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clauses 'other than as a consequence of a breach of an obligation of confidentiality to HALO' in section A and 'without breaching an obligation of confidentiality' in section D creates a loophole. It allows the recipient to disclose information even if it became public *because* they breached the NDA, or if they received it from a 3rd party who did breach the NDA.
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing critical qualifications regarding the source and context of publicly available information, the modified text weakens trade secret protection, potentially contradicting the intent of CUTSA. Under CUTSA, information that becomes public due to a breach of confidentiality is still protected.  This change would allow a recipient to claim information is non-confidential even if they caused its disclosure.

perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens trade secret protection, potentially contradicting the intent of CUTSA, as explained in the Law Explanation. The original text had clauses that ensured that the recipient's obligations to confidentiality were maintained even if the information became public or was received from a third party, so long as there was no breach of confidentiality involved. The changed text removes these qualifications, creating a loophole. The scraped snippets do not provide specific text directly violated, but confirm the existence of the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) in California (Snippet 1: 'This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act.'). Thus, by removing the qualifications surrounding breaches of confidentiality, the contract now allows the recipient to disclose information even if they caused its disclosure.

perturbation.original_text: All information furnished to RECIPIENT by HALO shall, unless otherwise specified in writing by HALO, remain the property of HALO; and the written information, and any copies thereof, shall be promptly returned to HALO upon its written or destroyed by RECIPIENT at HALO’s option.
perturbation.changed_text: All information furnished to RECIPIENT by HALO shall, unless otherwise specified in writing by HALO, become the property of RECIPIENT; and RECIPIENT can do whatever they want with it.
perturbation.explanation: Stating that the information becomes the property of the recipient contradicts the standard expectation in an NDA that the disclosing party retains ownership of the confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The change directly contradicts the principles underlying trade secret protection under CUTSA. By relinquishing ownership of the information, HALO effectively abandons any claim to trade secret status, as a key element is that the information must be subject to efforts to maintain its secrecy. The term 'trade secret' is defined under Cal. Civ. Code § 3426.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the principle of trade secret protection, as it explicitly states that the information becomes the property of the recipient, relinquishing HALO's ownership. According to the Law Explanation, 'By relinquishing ownership of the information, HALO effectively abandons any claim to trade secret status, as a key element is that the information must be subject to efforts to maintain its secrecy.' The scraped law snippet 1, Cal. Civ. Code § 3426, indicates that the title refers to the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. By giving away ownership, HALO cannot enforce its trade secret rights.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws (excluding the laws of conflict of laws) of the State of Califor nia.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws (including the laws of conflict of laws) of the State of Nevada.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to Nevada but including its conflict of laws provisions could lead to the application of another jurisdiction's laws if Nevada's conflict of laws rules dictate that. Also, a company headquartered in California should probably have the law of the state where their business is.
perturbation.law_explanation: While parties can generally choose the governing law, California courts may disregard the chosen law if it violates a strong public policy of California, especially if the agreement significantly impacts California residents or businesses. By changing to Nevada law, and including conflict of laws, which may result in another law being used, it undermines a reasonable expectation of where the law should be.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. INTERPRETATION OF CONTRACTS [1635 - 1663] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) 1646. A contract is to be interpreted according to the law and usage of the place where it is to be performed; or, if it does not indicate a place of performance, according to the law and usage of the place where it is made. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: ll contracts, whether public or private, are to be interpreted by the same rules, except as otherwise provided by this Code. 1636. A contract must be so interpreted as to give effect to the mutual intention of the parties as it existed at the time of contracting, so far as the same is ascertainable and lawful. 1637. For the purpose of ascertaining the intention of the parties to a contract, if otherwise doubtful, the rules given in this Chapter are to be applied. 1638. The language of a contract is to govern its interpretation, if the language is clear and explicit, and does not involve an absurdity. 1639. When a contract is reduced to writing, the intention of the parties is to be ascertained from the writing alone, if possible; subject, however, to the other provisions of this Title. 1640. When, through fraud, mistake, or accident, a written contract fails to express the real intention of the parties, such intention is to be regarded, and the erroneous parts of the writing disregarded. 1641. The whole of a contract is to be taken together, so as to give effect to every part, if reasonably practicable, each clause helping to interpret the other. 1642. Several contracts relating to the same matters, between the same parties, and made as parts of substantially one transaction, are to be taken together. 1643. A contract must receive such an interpretation as will make it lawful, operative, definite, reasonable, and capable of being carried into effect, if it can be done without violating the intention of the parties. 1644. The words of a contract are to be understood in their ordinary and popular sense, rather than according to their strict legal meaning; unless used by the parties in a technical sense, or unless a special meaning is given to them by usage, in which case the latter must be followed. 1645. Technical words are to be interpreted as usually understood by persons in the profession or business to which they relate, unless clearly used in a different sense. 1646. A contract is to be interpreted according to the law and usage of the place where it is to be performed; or, if it does not indicate a place of performance, according to the law and usage of the place where it is made. 1646.5. Notwithstanding Section 1646, the parties to any contract, agreement, or undertaking, contingent or otherwise, relating to a transaction involving in the aggregate not less than two hundred fifty thousand dollars ($250,000), including a transaction otherwise covered by subdivision (1) of Section 1105 of the Commercial Code, may agree that the law of this state shall govern their rights and duties in whole or in part, whether or not the contract, agreement, or undertaking or transaction bears a reasonable relation to this state. This section does not apply to any contract, agreement, or undertaking (a) for labor or personal services, (b) relating to any transaction primarily for personal, family, or household purposes, or (c) to the extent provided to the contrary in subdivision (2) of Section 1105 of the Commercial Code. This section applies to contracts, agreements, and undertakings entered into before, on, or after its effective date; it shall be fully retroactive. Contracts, agreements, and undertakings selecting California law entered into before the effective date of this section shall be valid, enforceable, and effective as if this section had been in effect on the date they were entered into; and actions and proceedings commencing in a court of this state before the effective date of this section may be maintained as if this section were in effect on the date they were commenced. 1647. A contract may be explained by reference to the circumstances under which it was made, and the matter to which it relates. 1648. However broad may be the terms of a contract, it extends only to those things concerning which it appears that the parties intended to contract. 1649. If the terms of a promise are in any respect ambiguous or uncertain, it mus
perturbation.justification: The change in governing law from California to Nevada, while including Nevada's conflict of laws provisions, creates a contradiction. While parties can generally choose the governing law, California courts may disregard the chosen law if it violates a strong public policy of California. By including conflict of laws, it could result in another law being used, undermining a reasonable expectation of where the law should be applied. Snippet 2, particularly CIV 1646, states that a contract is to be interpreted according to the law of the place where it is to be performed or made, which in the original contract pointed to California law. The altered contract could potentially circumvent this, creating a conflict and supporting the existence of a contradiction.
